title: Sampling & Statistics
blurb: Random samples, inference about a population, and comparing two data sets with center and spread.

lessons.title: Populations & Samples
lessons.concreteComparison: A soup chef doesn't drink the whole pot to check the seasoning — one stirred spoonful speaks for all of it. Pollsters don't ask every voter, and factories don't crash-test every car. The whole pot is the **population**; the spoonful is a **sample** — and the stir is what makes the spoonful trustworthy.
lessons.objective: Distinguish populations from samples, and explain when and why sampling replaces a census.
concept: The **population** is the entire group you want to know about — every student in the school, every battery off the line. A measurement of everyone is a **census**.
A **sample** is the subset you actually examine. Good samples let a few hundred stand in for millions.
Sampling wins when the population is too big (all voters), the testing destroys the item (crash tests, taste tests), or answers are needed fast.
A sample is only useful if it REPRESENTS the population — same mix, small size. The chef stirs the pot precisely so the spoonful matches the whole.
lessons.keyIdea: Population = everyone you care about; sample = who you actually asked; representation is what earns trust.

facts.title: Census vs sample
facts.explanation: A census measures all; a sample measures a representative few.

facts.title: When sampling wins
facts.explanation: You can't crash-test every car or interview every voter.

workedExamples.statement: A school of $1{,}200$ students surveys $80$ about lunch preferences. Identify the population and the sample.
workedExamples.solution: Population: all $1{,}200$ students. Sample: the $80$ surveyed. The sample is $\frac{80}{1200} = \frac{1}{15}$ of the population.

workedExamples.statement: A factory tests the lifespan of batteries by running them until they die. Census or sample — and why?
workedExamples.solution: Sample, necessarily: the test DESTROYS the battery. A census would leave nothing to sell.

workedExamples.statement: To estimate the average height of $30$ classmates, you measure all $30$. Census or sample?
workedExamples.solution: A census — the population is small enough to measure entirely, so no inference is needed.

commonMistakes.correction: The population is who you want conclusions ABOUT; the sample is who you actually measured. '80 students' is the sample; 'our school' is the population.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming bigger populations need proportionally bigger samples.
commonMistakes.correction: A well-chosen sample of ~1,000 can represent a country of millions. Representativeness beats raw size.

tryIt.statement: A biologist nets $150$ fish from a lake holding an unknown number. Name the population and sample.
tryIt.solution: Population: all fish in the lake; sample: the $150$ netted.

tryIt.statement: Give one reason a phone maker samples rather than testing every unit for drop damage.
tryIt.solution: Drop-testing damages the phone — destructive testing forces sampling.

steps.title: Pot and spoonful
beats: **Population**: the whole pot — everyone you care about.
**Sample**: the spoonful — those you measure.
**Census**: drinking the entire pot.
Statistics is the art of trusting spoonfuls.

steps.title: Three good reasons
beats: TOO BIG: every voter, every fish, every tweet.
DESTRUCTIVE: crash tests, taste tests, battery lifespans.
TOO SLOW: election night can't wait for a census.
One good spoonful beats an impossible pot-chug.

steps.title: Name the parts
steps.prompt: A journalist interviews $250$ of a city's $40{,}000$ commuters. The population is…
steps.explanation: The conclusions are about ALL commuters — that's the population; the 250 are the sample.

steps.title: Spot the structure
steps.intro: Who do we want to know about? Who did we ask?

examples.prompt: A game studio beta-tests with $2{,}000$ of its $3$ million players. Identify both parts and the fraction sampled.
Quality control pulls every $50$th jar off a conveyor of $10{,}000$ jars. How many jars get inspected?
When is a census actually the right tool?
steps: Small populations: your class, one shelf of stock.
When the answer must be exact (an election itself IS a census).
When measuring is cheap and harmless.

steps.title: Population radar
steps.intro: Find the whole pot and the spoonful.

problems.prompt: A vet clinic weighs $40$ of the town's roughly $6{,}000$ dogs. The sample is…
problems.explanation: The 40 measured dogs are the sample; the town's dogs are the population.

problems.prompt: Which situation FORCES sampling (a census is impossible)?
problems.explanation: A burned match is spent — destructive testing rules out a census.

problems.prompt: A survey of $500$ people speaks for a nation of $50$ million. What fraction was sampled?
problems.explanation: $\frac{500}{50000000} = \frac{1}{100000}$ — tiny, yet potentially trustworthy if chosen well.

steps.title: The magazine that asked 2 million people — and got it wrong
steps.body: In 1936, *Literary Digest* mailed 10 million straw ballots and received 2.4 million back — then confidently predicted the wrong U.S. president. Its mailing lists (car owners, telephone subscribers) skewed wealthy during the Depression. George Gallup called the election correctly with a sample 50 times smaller but properly mixed. Size lost to representativeness, publicly and forever.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Population = the whole group; sample = the measured subset; census = measuring all.
Sample when the population is huge, the test destroys, or time is short.
A sample earns trust by REPRESENTING the population, not by being big.

lessons.title: Random Samples & Bias
lessons.concreteComparison: Survey gym-goers about exercise habits and you'll conclude everyone loves working out. The gym door did the choosing — and it chose badly. A **biased** sample bakes the answer into the question. The fix is randomness: give every member of the population an EQUAL chance of being picked, and no door gets a vote.
lessons.objective: Recognize biased sampling methods and design simple random samples.
concept: A **random sample** gives every individual an equal chance of selection — names from a hat, random number generators, every-nth-person from a full list.
**Bias** creeps in whenever the method favors some group: surveying at the gym (exercise questions), online polls (only the motivated respond), asking friends (they resemble you).
Common bias flavors: **convenience** (whoever's nearby), **voluntary response** (whoever volunteers — usually the loudest opinions), and **undercoverage** (the method misses part of the population).
Randomness doesn't guarantee a perfect sample — it guarantees NO SYSTEMATIC tilt, and it lets the errors shrink as the sample grows.
lessons.keyIdea: Random = every member equally likely; bias = the method tilts the answer before anyone responds.

facts.title: Random sample
facts.explanation: Hat draws, random numbers, every-nth from a complete list.

facts.title: Bias signatures
facts.explanation: If the METHOD favors a group, the data tilts before you collect it.

workedExamples.statement: To gauge school-lunch opinions, a student surveys the first $30$ people in the pizza line. What's wrong?
workedExamples.solution: Convenience + undercoverage: pizza-line students likely like the lunch. Students who bring lunch from home never get asked.

workedExamples.statement: Design a random sample of $50$ from a school's $1{,}000$ students.
workedExamples.solution: Number the full roster $1$–$1000$; use a random number generator to pick $50$ distinct numbers. Every student has probability $\frac{50}{1000} = \frac{1}{20}$.

workedExamples.statement: A radio host asks listeners to call in about a new tax. $84\%$ of callers oppose it. Trustworthy?
workedExamples.solution: No — voluntary response: angry people call, content people don't. The 84% describes CALLERS, not the city.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking a big biased sample fixes itself.
commonMistakes.correction: 2.4 million tilted responses still tilt. Bias is a direction, not a noise — more of it just repeats the tilt louder.
Wandering the hallway asking 'random' people favors the outgoing and the nearby. Real randomness needs a mechanism — numbers, hats, lists.

tryIt.statement: Name the bias: a website's poll about screen-time limits, answered by whoever clicks.
tryIt.solution: Voluntary response (and undercoverage of people not on the site).

tryIt.statement: A quality engineer tests every $25$th phone from the full production run of $5{,}000$. How many are tested, and is the method sound?
tryIt.solution: $5000 \div 25 = 200$ tested. Systematic sampling from a COMPLETE list — sound.

steps.title: Bias picks the answer first
beats: Ask the gym about exercise → 'everyone exercises!'
The METHOD chose the answer, not the people.
Bias is a built-in tilt, invisible in the data itself.
You must inspect HOW the sample was gathered.

steps.title: Three faces to memorize
beats: **Convenience**: whoever's easiest to reach.
**Voluntary response**: whoever cares enough to answer — the loud.
**Undercoverage**: the method can't even see part of the population.
All three tilt before a single response arrives.

steps.title: Randomness has no favorites
beats: Equal chance for every member — that's the definition.
Tools: hat draws, random numbers, every-nth from a FULL list.
Random errors average out; biased errors accumulate.
No mechanism, no randomness.

steps.title: Diagnose it
steps.prompt: To predict a school election, Amir surveys his own soccer team. The main problem is…
options: convenience bias — teammates aren't the whole school
steps.explanation: His team is nearby and similar to him — a convenience sample that undercovers everyone else.

steps.title: Fix the method
steps.intro: Spot the tilt, then redesign with equal chances.

examples.prompt: A city wants park-usage data and surveys people IN the park. Diagnose and fix.
steps: Bias: park visitors love parks — undercoverage of non-visitors.
Fix: sample from the city's resident list at random.
Phone/mail random residents; the park stops choosing.

examples.prompt: A teacher wants feedback and asks for volunteers to stay after class. Diagnose and fix.
steps: Voluntary response: strong opinions stay, others leave.
Fix: pick names from the roster with a random draw.
Every student equally likely — the tilt disappears.

examples.prompt: From a numbered list of $800$ customers, every $16$th is surveyed. Sample size — and one caution.
steps: Sound IF the list order is unrelated to opinions.
Beware hidden patterns in the list (e.g., sorted by purchase size).

steps.title: Bias detective
steps.intro: Judge the method, not the numbers.

problems.prompt: Best method to sample $30$ of $600$ students?
options: Random numbers against the full roster
Whoever replies to a group chat
problems.explanation: Only the roster + random numbers gives everyone an equal chance.

problems.prompt: An online poll finds $91\%$ favor a controversial rule change. The most likely explanation is…
problems.explanation: Click-in polls attract the motivated; the silent majority never votes.

problems.prompt: Calling landline phones at 2 PM on weekdays undercovers…
problems.explanation: Anyone at work or without a landline can never enter the sample.

steps.title: Wheat, chessboards, and random digits
steps.body: Before computers, statisticians drew random samples using books of random digits — the RAND Corporation's *A Million Random Digits* (1955) was a bestseller among scientists and is still in print. Reviewers joked it was 'the book with a million typos, all intentional.' Today your phone generates the digits, but the principle is unchanged: mechanized fairness beats human 'randomness' every time.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Bias is a tilt built into the METHOD — convenience, volunteers, undercoverage.
Random sampling gives every member an equal chance via a real mechanism.
Big biased samples stay biased; random errors wash out.

lessons.title: Inference from Samples
lessons.concreteComparison: A biologist nets $60$ fish from a lake; $12$ are tagged from last year's release of $500$ tagged fish. If $\frac{12}{60} = \frac{1}{5}$ of the lake is tagged, and $500$ fish ARE the tagged ones, the lake holds about $500 \times 5 = 2{,}500$ fish. That leap — from spoonful to pot — is **inference**, and proportions are the ladder.
lessons.objective: Scale sample proportions up to population estimates, and recognize that estimates vary from sample to sample.
concept: **Inference** projects a sample's proportion onto the whole population: if $30\%$ of a random sample likes chess, estimate $30\%$ of the population does.
Estimated count $=$ sample proportion $\times$ population size: $\frac{18}{60} \times 1200 = 360$ students.
The **capture–recapture** trick estimates invisible populations: tag some, resample, and set the tagged proportion equal in sample and lake.
Different random samples give DIFFERENT estimates — that wobble is normal. Larger samples wobble less; a range of plausible values beats false precision.
lessons.keyIdea: Sample proportion × population size = estimate; expect sample-to-sample wobble, shrinking as samples grow.

facts.title: Scale up
facts.explanation: The sample's fraction, applied to the whole population.

facts.title: Capture–recapture
facts.explanation: Two views of the same proportion — solve for the unknown lake.

workedExamples.statement: In a random sample of $60$ students, $18$ walk to school. Estimate the walkers among all $1{,}200$ students.
$500$ tagged fish are released. Later, $12$ of a $60$-fish sample are tagged. Estimate the lake's population.
Two random samples of $50$ shoppers find $14$ and $19$ who prefer the new logo. What do you conclude?
workedExamples.solution: Estimates: $28\%$ and $38\%$. The truth is plausibly around $\sim 33\%$ — sample wobble is normal; a bigger sample would narrow it.

commonMistakes.correction: "Exactly 360 students walk" overclaims. The honest phrasing: ABOUT 360, based on this sample.
Inference launders nothing: 30% of a gym sample scaled to the city just spreads the bias city-wide. Randomness first, inference second.

tryIt.statement: A random sample: $9$ of $45$ customers want longer hours. Estimate the count among $2{,}000$ customers.
$80$ tagged deer; a later sample finds $4$ tagged among $50$. Estimate the herd.

steps.title: Proportion, meet population
beats: Random sample: $18$ of $60$ walk to school — $30\%$.
Population: $1{,}200$ students.
Estimate: $0.30 \times 1200 = 360$ walkers.
Sample fraction × population = inference.

steps.title: Capture–recapture
beats: Tag $500$ fish; release; let them mix.
Recatch $60$; find $12$ tagged.
$\frac{12}{60} = \frac{500}{N}$ — same proportion, two views.
$N = 2500$. You counted a lake without draining it.

steps.title: Estimates wobble
beats: Two samples, two answers: $28\%$ vs $38\%$ — normal.
Wobble shrinks as samples grow.
Say ABOUT, give a range when you can.
Precision you didn't earn is just confidence, misspelled.

steps.title: Scale it
steps.prompt: A random sample of $80$ voters shows $52$ support a measure. Estimate supporters among $15{,}000$ voters.

examples.prompt: A random check finds $3$ defective toys in a sample of $120$. Estimate defects in a shipment of $8{,}000$.
A ranger tags $150$ trout. A later sample of $90$ contains $9$ tagged. Estimate the stream population.
Three random samples of $100$ students give $23, 27, 25$ bus riders. Give your best population statement for the school of $2{,}000$.

steps.title: Inference reps
steps.intro: Fraction first, then multiply — and stay humble.

problems.prompt: $21$ of a random $70$ prefer digital books. Estimated preference in a town of $4{,}000$ readers?
$200$ tagged birds; a sample of $40$ has $5$ tagged. Estimated flock size?
Which change most improves an estimate's reliability?
problems.explanation: Bigger random samples wobble less; bigger biased samples just repeat the tilt.

steps.title: Counting the uncountable
steps.body: Capture–recapture doesn't just count fish: epidemiologists use it to estimate unreported disease cases, linguists to estimate how many words Shakespeare knew but never wrote (about 35,000!), and software teams to estimate bugs left in code — 'tag' bugs found by tester A, see how many tester B 'recaptures.' One proportion equation, a dozen sciences.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Capture–recapture equates the tagged proportions in sample and population.
Estimates wobble between samples — bigger random samples wobble less.

lessons.title: Mean, Median & Outliers
lessons.concreteComparison: Nine employees earn around \$50{,}000; the CEO earns \$1{,}000{,}000. The **mean** salary is about \$145{,}000 — a number NOBODY earns. The **median** says \$50{,}000 — what a typical person actually makes. Two honest centers, one skewed dataset: which number tells the truth depends on the question.
lessons.objective: Compute mean and median for rational data, and judge which center to trust when outliers strike.
concept: The **mean** adds everything and divides by the count — every value pulls on it, so one giant value drags it far.
The **median** is the middle of the SORTED list (average the two middles for an even count) — it only cares about position, so outliers barely budge it.
An **outlier** is a value far from the pack. Means chase outliers; medians ignore them.
Rule of thumb: symmetric data → mean and median agree, use either; skewed data or outliers → the median describes 'typical' better. Incomes, house prices, and follower counts are median territory.
lessons.keyIdea: Mean = balance point (outlier-sensitive); median = middle position (outlier-resistant). Pick by the data's shape.

facts.title: Mean
facts.explanation: The balance point — every value pulls.

facts.title: Median
facts.explanation: Position-based, so outliers barely move it.

workedExamples.statement: Find the mean and median of $4, 7, 2, 9, 3$.
Quiz scores: $82, 85, 88, 90, 15$. Compute both centers; which describes a typical score?
workedExamples.solution: Mean: $\frac{360}{5} = 72$. Median: sorted $15, 82, 85, 88, 90 \to 85$. The median — the 15 is an outlier dragging the mean below every other score.

commonMistakes.correction: The median of 4, 7, 2, 9, 3 is NOT 2 (the middle of the unsorted list). Sort first: 2 3 4 7 9 → median 4.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting the mean for skewed data without comment.
commonMistakes.correction: 'Average salary \$145k' at the CEO's company misleads everyone. With outliers, report the median or both.

tryIt.statement: Find the mean and median of $-3, 5, 1, -1, 8$.
Data: $12, 14, 13, 15, 90$. Which center better describes a typical value, and why?
tryIt.solution: Median ($14$): the $90$ is an outlier that inflates the mean to $\frac{144}{5} = 28.8$.

steps.title: The mean: balance point
beats: Add everything, divide by the count.
Physically: where the dot plot balances on a fulcrum.
Every value pulls — including monsters.

steps.title: The median: middle seat
beats: SORT first. Then take the middle.
Even count? Average the two middles.
Only position matters — a 90 or a 90,000 at the end changes nothing.

steps.title: When outliers attack
beats: Salaries: nine at \$50k, one at \$1M.
Skewed data? Trust the middle seat, not the balance point.

steps.title: Which center?
steps.prompt: House prices on a street: eight near \$200k, one mansion at \$3M. Which statistic best describes a typical house?
steps.explanation: The mansion drags the mean far above every normal house; the median stays at a typical price.

steps.title: Centers under pressure
steps.intro: Compute both; let the shape pick the spokesperson.

examples.prompt: Salaries: nine at \$50{,}000 and one at \$1{,}000{,}000. Verify the mean.

steps: Symmetric data — the centers agree; use either.

examples.prompt: A dataset's mean is $50$ across $8$ values. A 9th value of $140$ arrives. New mean?
steps: New mean: $60$ — one value moved it 10 points.

steps.title: Center check
steps.intro: Sort for medians; watch for monsters.

problems.prompt: Removing an outlier of $95$ from $\{10, 12, 11, 95\}$ changes the mean from $32$ to…
problems.explanation: $\frac{33}{3} = 11$ — the mean falls by two-thirds when the monster leaves.

steps.title: The billionaire walks into a bar
steps.body: Statisticians joke: a billionaire walks into a bar and the MEAN patron becomes a millionaire — while the MEDIAN patron still can't afford the fancy beer. Nearly every 'average income rose' headline quietly uses the mean; nearly every 'typical family' claim should use the median. Knowing which was used is half of statistical literacy.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Mean = sum ÷ count; median = middle of the sorted list.
Outliers drag means and barely touch medians.
Skewed data (incomes, prices): report the median — or both.

lessons.title: Range & Mean Absolute Deviation
lessons.concreteComparison: Two pizza shops both average $30$-minute deliveries. Shop A arrives in $28$–$32$ minutes, every time. Shop B swings between $10$ and $55$. Same center, wildly different EXPERIENCE. Centers can't see this — you need a **spread** number, and Grade 7's precision tool is the mean absolute deviation (MAD).
lessons.objective: Measure spread with range and MAD, and interpret MAD as the typical distance from the mean.
concept: **Range** $=$ max $-$ min: quick, but it only sees the two most extreme values.
**MAD** (mean absolute deviation) asks: on average, how far is a data point from the mean? Compute the mean, find each $|x - \text{mean}|$, then average those distances.
A small MAD means the data hugs its mean (consistent); a large MAD means it sprawls (unpredictable).
MAD's units are the data's units: 'deliveries typically miss the 30-minute mark by about 12 minutes' is a sentence customers understand.
lessons.keyIdea: Range = span of extremes; MAD = the average distance from the mean — the consistency meter.

facts.title: Range
facts.explanation: Fast, but blind to everything between the extremes.

facts.title: MAD
facts.explanation: The typical miss: average distance from the mean.

workedExamples.statement: Shop A's deliveries: $28, 29, 30, 31, 32$ minutes. Find the mean and MAD.
Shop B's deliveries: $10, 20, 30, 40, 50$ minutes. Find the MAD and compare with Shop A.
workedExamples.solution: Mean: $30$. Distances: $20, 10, 0, 10, 20$ — MAD $= 12$ minutes. Ten times Shop A's $1.2$: same center, far less consistency.

workedExamples.statement: Find the range of both shops' data.

commonMistakes.text: Averaging the deviations WITHOUT absolute value.
commonMistakes.correction: Raw deviations always sum to zero — the mean is the balance point. The absolute-value bars are what make MAD nonzero and meaningful.

commonMistakes.text: Comparing MADs of datasets with different units or scales.
commonMistakes.correction: A MAD of 5 cm and a MAD of 5 dollars aren't comparable. MAD speaks the data's units — compare like with like.

tryIt.statement: Data: $6, 8, 10, 12, 14$. Find the mean and the MAD.
Which is more consistent: quiz scores with MAD $2.1$ or MAD $6.8$?
tryIt.solution: MAD $2.1$ — scores typically sit only 2.1 points from their mean.

steps.title: Same mean, different worlds
beats: Both shops average $30$ minutes.
The mean can't tell them apart.
Spread is the missing coordinate.

steps.title: Range: the quick glance
beats: $\text{range} = \max - \min$.
A: $4$ minutes. B: $40$ minutes.
One subtraction — but only the extremes vote.
A single outlier can inflate it wildly.

steps.title: MAD: the typical miss
beats: Step 1: find the mean.
Step 2: each point's distance $|x - \bar{x}|$.
Step 3: average the distances.
MAD $= 1.2$ min: 'deliveries typically miss by about a minute.'

examples.prompt: A gymnast's scores: $9.0, 9.2, 9.4, 9.4$. Find the MAD.
Two classes' test MADs: Class X $= 4$, Class Y $= 11$, same mean $75$. Interpret.
steps: X: scores typically within $4$ of $75$ — tight, similar mastery.
Y: typical miss of $11$ — a wide mix of strong and struggling.
Every distance from the mean is $0$.

problems.prompt: Which forecast is easier to plan around: daily temps with MAD $1.5°$ or MAD $9°$?
problems.explanation: Small MAD = days hug the average — pack one jacket, not four.
$50 - 10 = 40$ — one outlier owns the whole range.

steps.title: Factories run on spread
steps.body: Modern manufacturing's 'Six Sigma' movement is an obsession with shrinking spread: a bottling line that averages 500 ml is useless if bottles swing between 430 and 570. Engineers track deviation charts hour by hour, and a rising spread halts the line even when the MEAN is perfect. In quality control, consistency isn't a virtue — it's the product.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Range = max − min: fast but extremes-only.
MAD = average distance from the mean — the typical miss, in the data's units.
Same mean, different MAD = very different experiences.

lessons.title: Comparing Two Populations
lessons.concreteComparison: Team Falcon averages $52$ points per game; Team Hawk averages $58$. Is Hawk really better — or is that $6$-point gap just noise? It depends on the WOBBLE: if both teams' scores typically vary by $3$ points (MAD), a $6$-point gap is two full wobbles — real. If they vary by $15$, the gap is a shrug. Comparing groups means comparing gaps to spreads.
lessons.objective: Compare two data sets using their centers and spreads, judging differences as multiples of the MAD.
concept: To compare two groups, compare their **centers** (means or medians) — but always WITH their **spreads** in view.
The yardstick: express the gap between means as a multiple of the MAD. Gap $\ge 2$ MADs → the groups genuinely differ; gap under $1$ MAD → heavy overlap, weak evidence.
Visual version: two dot plots that barely overlap show a real difference; plots that mostly overlap don't — no matter which mean is bigger.
Remember the source: if the data came from samples, sampling wobble stacks on top. Bigger samples make comparisons trustworthy.
lessons.keyIdea: A difference in means matters when it's large COMPARED TO the spread — count it in MADs.

facts.title: The yardstick
facts.explanation: 2+ MADs of separation = a visible, meaningful difference.

facts.title: Overlap test
facts.explanation: Mostly-overlapping plots don't, whatever the means say.

workedExamples.statement: Falcon: mean $52$, MAD $3$. Hawk: mean $58$, MAD $3$. Compare the teams.
workedExamples.solution: Gap: $6$ points $= \frac{6}{3} = 2$ MADs. Two full typical-wobbles apart — Hawk's advantage is real.

workedExamples.statement: Plant food X: mean height $24$ cm, MAD $5$. Plant food Y: mean $26$ cm, MAD $5$. Compare.
workedExamples.solution: Gap: $2$ cm $= \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$ MADs. The distributions mostly overlap — weak evidence of any real difference.

workedExamples.statement: Class A quiz: mean $80$, MAD $4$. Class B: mean $70$, MAD $4$. How separated are they?

commonMistakes.text: Declaring a winner from means alone.
commonMistakes.correction: 58 beats 52 only if the spread is small. A 6-point gap inside a 15-point typical wobble is noise wearing a trophy.

commonMistakes.text: Comparing gaps in absolute terms across contexts.
commonMistakes.correction: A 6-point gap can be enormous (MAD 2) or trivial (MAD 20). Only the gap-to-MAD ratio travels between problems.

tryIt.statement: Group P: mean $40$, MAD $6$. Group Q: mean $52$, MAD $6$. Express the gap in MADs.
Two sleep studies: means $7.1$ and $7.3$ hours, both MAD $0.9$. Meaningful difference?
tryIt.solution: Gap $0.2 \approx 0.22$ MADs — heavy overlap; not meaningful.

steps.title: Bigger — or just noisier?
beats: Hawk 58, Falcon 52: a 6-point gap.
Every team's scores wobble game to game.
Is 6 large compared to the wobble?
That's the ONLY comparison that means anything.

steps.title: Measure gaps in MADs
beats: Gap $\div$ MAD $=$ separations.
$\frac{6}{3} = 2$ MADs → distributions barely overlap. Real.
$\frac{2}{5} = 0.4$ MADs → mostly overlap. Shrug.
Rule of thumb: 2+ MADs is a difference you can see.

steps.title: Dot plots don't lie
beats: Draw both groups on one number line.
Barely-touching clouds → genuine difference.
Heavily overlapping clouds → the means' order is luck.
The picture and the MAD-count always agree.

steps.title: Real or noise?
steps.prompt: Two keyboards: mean typing speeds $62$ and $68$ wpm, both with MAD $2$. Verdict?

examples.prompt: Morning class: mean $74$, MAD $8$. Afternoon: mean $78$, MAD $8$. Compare honestly.
Brand L bulbs: mean life $1200$ h, MAD $50$. Brand M: mean $1450$ h, MAD $50$. Compare.
steps: Massive separation — Brand M genuinely lasts longer.

examples.prompt: Two runners' 5K times (minutes): A $\{22, 23, 24\}$, B $\{20, 25, 30\}$. Same mean — who's more dependable?
steps: Both means: $23$ and $25$… compute: A mean $23$, B mean $25$.
A is far more consistent; B is faster on a good day and slower on a bad one.

steps.title: Judge the gap
steps.intro: Divide the gap by the MAD before declaring winners.

problems.prompt: Gap between means: $9$; MAD of both groups: $3$. Separation in MADs?
Which pair shows the STRONGEST evidence of a real difference?
Two groups have identical means. Their MADs are $2$ and $12$. What differs?
options: consistency — one group is far more variable
problems.explanation: Same center, different spread: one group is steady, the other swings.

steps.title: How medicine decides
steps.body: Every drug trial is exactly this lesson at industrial scale: the treatment group's mean against the placebo group's mean, judged against the spread. A blood-pressure drop of 4 points 'sounds small' — but if patient-to-patient variation is tiny, it's a breakthrough; if variation is huge, it's noise. The gap-to-spread ratio (statisticians call theirs an *effect size*) is arguably the most consequential fraction in science.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Compare groups by centers AND spreads together.
Gap ÷ MAD is the yardstick: 2+ MADs = visibly real.
Same means, different MADs = a consistency story, not a winner story.

practice.statement: A librarian surveys $45$ of the library's $900$ members. Name the population, the sample, and the sampled fraction.
practice.solution: Population: all $900$ members; sample: the $45$; fraction: $\frac{45}{900} = \frac{1}{20}$.

practice.statement: Identify the bias: polling about homework load using volunteers from the after-school study club.
practice.solution: Voluntary response plus undercoverage — study-club members aren't typical, and volunteers self-select.

practice.statement: A random sample finds $12$ of $48$ students bike to school. Estimate bikers among $1{,}600$ students.
$300$ tagged salmon; a later sample of $75$ contains $9$ tagged. Estimate the run size.
Find the mean and median of $-2, 4, 7, 1, 5$.
Two farms' pumpkin weights: means $9$ kg and $12$ kg, both MAD $1.5$. Compare in MADs.
Salaries: $40, 42, 44, 46, 200$ (thousands). Give both centers and say which describes 'typical'.
practice.solution: Mean: $\frac{372}{5} = 74.4$k; median: $44$k. The median — the $200$k outlier hijacks the mean.

testYourself.statement: Design a random sample of $40$ from $800$ concert attendees, and state each person's selection probability.
testYourself.solution: Number ticket stubs $1$–$800$; draw $40$ distinct random numbers. Each attendee: $\frac{40}{800} = \frac{1}{20}$.

testYourself.statement: A mall survey near the food court finds $70\%$ 'love mall food.' Explain the flaw in one sentence.
testYourself.solution: Convenience/undercoverage bias: people at the food court already chose mall food — the method preloaded the answer.

testYourself.statement: In a random sample, $33$ of $150$ households compost. Estimate composters among $12{,}000$ households, and phrase it honestly.
testYourself.solution: $\frac{33}{150} = 0.22$: about $2{,}640$ households — an estimate, not an exact count.

testYourself.statement: Reaction times (ms): $220, 250, 230, 300, 250$. Find the mean and MAD.
Which shows a REAL difference: means $55$ vs $59$ with MADs $2$, or means $55$ vs $70$ with MADs $20$?
testYourself.solution: First: gap $4 = 2$ MADs — real. Second: gap $15 = 0.75$ MADs — overlap. The FIRST, despite the smaller gap.

testYourself.statement: A dataset of $6$ values has mean $18$. Five of the values are $15, 16, 19, 21, 22$. Find the sixth.
testYourself.solution: Sum must be $108$; known five sum to $93$; sixth $= 15$.
